title: 1 Timothy 6
subtitle: Contentment, the Good Fight, and Final Charge

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — "All Who Are Under the Yoke of Slavery"

heb.paragraph: The medical metaphor ὑγιαίνω (hygiaino, to be healthy) applied to doctrine is a Pastorals signature: teaching is either ‘healthy’ (ὑγιαίνω) or ‘sick’ (νοσέω, v. 4). BDAG gives ‘to be correct in one’s views.’ The metaphor diagnoses false teaching as a disease of the mind that infects the community—a powerful rhetorical strategy that pathologizes heresy and positions sound doctrine as the cure.

refs.note: Paul’s fuller slave instructions in the earlier epistles provide the theological framework (serving as to the Lord) that this briefer text presupposes.

The parallel slave instruction in Titus explicitly adds the motivation ‘so that in every way they will make the teaching about God our Savior attractive’—the missionary rationale underlying both passages.

notes.note: Christian slaves must honor their masters so that God’s name and the gospel are not slandered. Slaves with believing masters should serve even better, because those who benefit from their service are fellow believers.

The false teacher’s profile is clear: he disagrees with the sound words of Christ, is conceited, knows nothing, has a morbid interest in controversies and disputes, and thinks godliness is a means of financial gain. Withdraw from such people.

Slaves must serve faithfully so as not to bring reproach on the gospel. Those who use religion to undermine social order do more harm than good. The false teachers are diagnosed as sick (νοσῶν): their love of controversy is a spiritual pathology, not legitimate inquiry, and their exploitation of religion for profit reveals corrupt hearts.

The slave instruction assumes the institution of slavery without endorsing it. The concern is pragmatic and missionary: preventing the gospel from being associated with social revolution that would invite imperial suppression.
The verb νοσέω (to be sick) creates a medical metaphor: the false teacher is ‘diseased’ with a craving for controversy, in contrast to the ‘healthy’ (ὑγιαίνω) words of sound doctrine.

The slave instruction is brief because the primary concern is not social reform but reputation: God’s name and ἡ διδασκαλία (the teaching) must not be βλασφημῆται (blasphemed/slandered). The exhortation to slaves with believing masters is a nuanced social observation: the gospel’s leveling effect (Gal 3:28) could be misread as license to disrespect a master who is now a ‘brother.’

The false teacher is characterized by a cluster of intellectual and moral deficits: τετύφωται (puffed up with conceit), μηδὲν ἐπιστάμενος (understanding nothing), and νοσῶν (diseased) with controversy. The diagnosis is devastating: theological incompetence masked by intellectual pretension, generating only φθόνος (envy), ἔρις (strife), βλασφημίαι (slander), and ὑπόνοιαι πονηραί (evil suspicions).

The slave instruction serves a dual purpose: it maintains social order in a context where Christian egalitarian impulses could provoke hostile attention, and it models the obedient responsiveness that the letter commends throughout. The transition to false teachers (vv. 3–5) implies a connection: the heretics may have encouraged social disruption (slaves challenging masters) as part of their deviant teaching. The medical language (νοσῶν, ὑγιαίνω) pathologizes the opponents’ intellectual posture: where sound doctrine produces social harmony, diseased teaching generates communal conflict.

hist.context: The slave instruction (vv. 1–2) is the shortest of the Pastoral household-code sections and reflects the social reality that many early Christians were enslaved. The transition from slaves (vv. 1–2) to false teachers (vv. 3–5) is abrupt but thematically linked: both address the danger of bringing the gospel into disrepute through inappropriate behavior. The false teachers’ ‘morbid craving for controversy’ (νοσῶν περὶ ζητήσεις, v. 4) contrasts sharply with the ‘sound words’ of Christ.

sections.header: Verses 6–10 — "Godliness with Contentment Is Great Gain"

heb.paragraph: A term borrowed from Stoic philosophy, αὐτάρκεια (autarkeia) originally meant self-sufficiency—the ability to live independently of external circumstances. Paul Christianizes the concept: true contentment comes not from Stoic detachment but from trust in God’s provision. The same word appears in 2 Cor 9:8 and Phil 4:11, where Paul claims to have learned contentment in all circumstances through Christ who strengthens him.

A compound noun from φίλος (loving) and ἄργυρος (silver/money), φιλαργυρία appears only here in the NT. Paul calls it the root (ῥίζα) of all kinds of evil—not ‘all evil’ absolutely but πάντων τῶν κακῶν, ‘all the evils’ (referring to the specific evils just listed). Ancient moralists widely condemned love of money; Paul shares this universal ethical conviction while grounding it in faith: craving money leads to wandering from the faith.

refs.note: Paul’s personal testimony of contentment in all circumstances provides the experiential complement to the theological teaching here.
Jesus’ teaching that one cannot serve both God and money and that treasures should be stored in heaven provides the dominical foundation for Paul’s anti-avarice ethic.
The Preacher’s observation that those who love money never have enough parallels Paul’s warning about the insatiable nature of financial desire.

notes.note: Godliness with contentment is great gain. We brought nothing into the world, and we can take nothing out. If we have food and clothing, we will be content with that. The simplicity of this standard rebukes our materialism.

Those who want to get rich fall into temptation and a trap and into many foolish and harmful desires that plunge people into ruin and destruction. The love of money is a root of all kinds of evil. Some have wandered from the faith and pierced themselves with many griefs.

Paul transforms the secular concept of self-sufficiency into a theological virtue: true contentment rests not in having much but in needing little, because God provides what is necessary. The love of money is not just a moral failing but a spiritual cancer that leads people away from faith. Avarice is the parent of every evil because it sets the creature’s desire above the Creator’s provision.
The saying about bringing nothing into and taking nothing from the world has close parallels in Job 1:21 and Eccl 5:15, as well as in Stoic philosophy (Seneca, Ep. 102.24–25).
The NIV’s 'love of money is a root of all kinds of evil' correctly translates ῥίζα πάντων τῶν κακῶν as ‘a root of all the evils’ (the evils just described), not ‘the root of all evil’ universally.

The εὐσέβεια μετὰ αὐταρκείας (godliness with contentment) formula redefines the opponents’ commercial πορισμός (means of gain, v. 5) in non-material terms. The αὐτάρκεια is Christianized Stoic vocabulary: not Stoic self-sufficiency but God-dependent sufficiency.

The compound metaphor of temptation → trap → desires → ruin traces the progression from initial attraction to final destruction. The φιλαργυρία saying is proverbial in form but contextually targeted: the false teachers’ greed is not incidental but diagnostic of their spiritual condition.

The contentment ethic engages both the opponents and the wider Greco-Roman moral discourse. The term αὐτάρκεια would resonate with any educated Ephesian familiar with Stoic and Cynic ideals, but Paul fills it with theistic content: contentment is a response to divine provision, not an achievement of rational self-mastery. The money warning targets the false teachers’ economic exploitation while also addressing a universal human vulnerability.

hist.context: Paul’s teaching on money and contentment draws on widespread Greco-Roman philosophical traditions (Stoicism, Cynicism) while recasting them in a theological key. The saying ‘we brought nothing into the world, and we can take nothing out of it’ (v. 7) echoes Job 1:21, Ecclesiastes 5:15, and Seneca’s Epistles. The critique of those who ‘want to get rich’ (v. 9) specifically targets the false teachers’ financial exploitation (cf. Tit 1:11, ‘teaching for dishonest gain’), linking avarice to heresy.

sections.header: Verses 11–16 — "Fight the Good Fight of the Faith"

heb.paragraph: The phrase combines the verb ἀγωνίζομαι (agonizomai, to compete, to contend) with the cognate noun ἀγών (agōn, contest). Originally athletic terminology for competing in the games, it acquired metaphorical use for any strenuous exertion. Paul uses the same root in 2 Tim 4:7 (‘I have fought the good fight’), creating an inclusio across the two letters: the charge Timothy receives in 1 Timothy, Paul claims to have fulfilled in 2 Timothy.

refs.note: Paul’s farewell declaration that he has ‘fought the good fight, finished the race, kept the faith’ completes the charge begun here—Timothy must now carry the baton.
Moses is the archetypal ‘man of God’ in the OT, and Paul’s use of this title for Timothy consciously places him in that prophetic lineage.
Jesus’ good confession before Pontius Pilate, mentioned in v. 13, is the christological model for Timothy’s own faithful witness.

notes.note: Flee from the love of money and pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, endurance, and gentleness. Fight the good fight of the faith. Take hold of the eternal life to which you were called. This is the Christian’s marching orders.

In the sight of God who gives life to everything, and of Christ Jesus who testified the good confession before Pilate, Paul charges Timothy to keep the commandment spotless and irreproachable until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ.

The doxology is magnificent: God is the blessed and only Ruler, the King of kings and Lord of lords, who alone is immortal and who lives in unapproachable light. No one has seen or can see him. To him be honor and might forever.

Paul calls Timothy a man of God—a title of prophetic dignity—and charges him to flee avarice and pursue virtue with the energy of an athlete. The good confession before Pilate becomes the pattern for Timothy’s own faithfulness under pressure. The doxology declares God’s transcendent majesty: the only Sovereign, immortal, dwelling in unapproachable light. Such a God deserves total obedience.
The phrase 'good confession' (την καλην ὁμολογίαν) likely refers to Timothy’s baptismal or ordination confession of faith, witnessed by the congregation (the ‘many witnesses’).
The language of ‘unapproachable light’ (φῶς οἰκῶν ἀπρόσιτον) draws on Hellenistic Jewish theophanic traditions (cf. Exod 33:20; Ps 104:2) and parallels language found in Jewish mystical texts.

The ἄνθρωπε θεοῦ (man of God) title consciously echoes the OT prophetic designation (Moses, Elijah, Elisha, Samuel). The six virtues to pursue (δικαιοσύνη, εὐσέβεια, πίστις, ἀγάπη, ὑπομονή, πραϋπαθία) replace the vices just condemned, forming a deliberate ethical contrast. The athletic metaphor (ἀγωνίζου) frames the Christian life as a contest requiring sustained effort.

The charge formula invokes both God and Christ as witnesses, with Christ’s confession before Pilate serving as the paradigmatic act of faithful testimony under pressure. The doxology uses language that both affirms Jewish monotheism (μόνος δυνάστης, sole Sovereign) and critiques imperial pretensions: βασιλεὺς τῶν βασιλευόντων (King of kings) is a direct counter-claim to Roman imperial theology.

The prophetic title ἄνθρωπε θεοῦ elevates Timothy’s role beyond organizational management to prophetic succession. The reference to Christ’s good confession before Pilate introduces a martyrological dimension: Timothy’s faithfulness may require the same kind of courageous witness in hostile circumstances. The doxology functions as both praise and political theology: by ascribing βασιλεύς τῶν βασιλευόντων to Israel’s God, Paul relativizes every earthly ruler—including Caesar.

hist.context: The direct address ὦ ἄνθρωπε θεοῦ (O man of God, v. 11) is an OT title used for Moses, Samuel, Elijah, and other prophetic figures. By applying it to Timothy, Paul locates him in the prophetic succession and elevates his charge beyond mere organizational management to covenantal responsibility. The doxology in vv. 15–16 is one of the most exalted descriptions of God in the Pauline corpus, using language drawn from Hellenistic Jewish liturgy.

sections.header: Verses 17–21 — "Guard What Has Been Entrusted to Your Care"

heb.paragraph: The noun παραθήκη (parathēkē, deposit, trust) is a legal-commercial term for goods or money entrusted to another for safekeeping (cf. 2 Tim 1:12, 14). Paul applies it to the apostolic teaching: the gospel is not Timothy’s to modify but a sacred trust to be guarded intact and transmitted faithfully. This concept of a fixed ‘deposit of faith’ becomes foundational in patristic theology and is central to debates about tradition and development of doctrine.

refs.note: The deposit language reappears in the second letter, where Paul expresses confidence that God will guard what has been entrusted to him, and charges Timothy to guard it through the Holy Spirit.
Jesus’ parable of the rich fool illustrates the danger of placing hope in material wealth rather than in God—the same warning Paul issues to the wealthy here.

notes.note: Command those who are rich not to be arrogant or to put their hope in wealth, which is so uncertain. Instead, they should put their hope in God, who richly provides us with everything for our enjoyment. They should be rich in good deeds, generous, and willing to share.
Guard the deposit entrusted to you. Turn away from godless chatter and the opposing ideas of what is falsely called knowledge. The gospel is a sacred trust—protect it with your life.

The rich are not condemned for their wealth but for trusting in it. God gives abundance to be enjoyed and shared—not hoarded. The closing charge to guard the deposit summarizes the entire letter: Timothy’s primary duty is to preserve and transmit the apostolic teaching without addition, subtraction, or distortion.

The phrase 'what is falsely called knowledge' (τῆς ψευδωνύμου γνώσεως) is sometimes read as a reference to early Gnosticism, though the term may simply describe the opponents’ pretentious claims to superior knowledge without formal Gnostic content.

The rich-instruction is distinguished from the anti-avarice polemic of vv. 9–10: those who are already wealthy are not told to divest but to reorient their trust from πλούτου ἀδηλότητι (uncertain riches) to the living God. The instruction to be κοινωνικούς (generous, sharing) transforms private wealth into community resource.

The παραθήκη (deposit) concept anchors the letter’s entire anti-heresy program: the apostolic teaching is a fixed trust to be guarded (φύλαξον), not a living tradition to be creatively developed. The ψευδώνυμος γνῶσις (falsely named knowledge) need not refer to second-century Gnosticism proper; it targets the Ephesian teachers’ claim to superior insight that in fact contradicts the apostolic deposit.

The closing charge weaves together three strands: the ethical instruction to the wealthy (a positive alternative to avarice), the deposit metaphor (summarizing Timothy’s custodial responsibility), and the final warning against false knowledge. The παραθήκη (deposit) concept is theologically significant: it implies a stable body of teaching received from the apostle that can be ‘guarded’ intact—a concept that will become central to early Catholic ecclesiology but that here functions within a living apostle-delegate relationship.

hist.context: The closing charge to the wealthy (vv. 17–19) addresses a different audience from the warning against those who ‘want to get rich’ (v. 9)—here Paul addresses those who are already rich. The counsel is not to renounce wealth but to use it generously and to ground their hope in God rather than in uncertain riches. The final warning against ‘falsely called knowledge’ (τῆς ψευδωνύμου γνώσεως, v. 20) may be the earliest reference to what would later become known as Gnosticism, though the term is used loosely.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks in the magnificent doxology (vv. 15–16). Faith and Mission frame the charge to fight the good fight and guard the deposit. Justice surfaces in the economic ethics and slave instructions.

rows.label: vv.11–16
rows.text: Charge to fight the good fight + doxology

lit.note: Chapter 6 concludes the letter with a sweep from social instruction (slaves, vv. 1–2) through polemics (vv. 3–5), ethics (vv. 6–10), personal charge (vv. 11–16), and a final word to the wealthy (vv. 17–19) before the climactic deposit-guarding exhortation (vv. 20–21).

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

ppl.name: Christ Jesus
ppl.text: In the sight of God who gives life to everything, and of Christ Jesus who testified the good confession before Pilate, Paul charges Timothy to keep the commandment spotless and irreproachable until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ…

thread.anchor: Contentment, the Good Fight, and Final Charge
thread.text: Paul’s fuller slave instructions in the earlier epistles provide the theological framework (serving as to the Lord) that this briefer text presupposes.

thread.anchor: Contentment, the Good Fight, and Final Charge
thread.text: Paul’s farewell declaration that he has ‘fought the good fight, finished the race, kept the faith’ completes the charge begun here—Timothy must now carry the baton.

coaching.tip: 'Godliness with contentment is great gain' (v. 6). The equation subverts acquisition culture: more stuff doesn't equal more gain. 'We brought nothing into the world, and we can take nothing out' (v. 7). The math is simple; we forget it constantly.

'Fight the good fight of the faith' (v. 12). Military imagery for spiritual perseverance. Timothy must 'take hold of eternal life' actively—not passive reception but engaged grip. Faith battles; neutrality loses.
